items.fix: Always maintain a `visited` set and skip already-seen nodes.
items.why: Without it the worklist will revisit nodes in cycles, causing exponential blowup or infinite loops.
items.example:
```python
visited = set()
for nb in graph[node]:
    if nb in visited: continue
    visited.add(nb)
    worklist.append(nb)
```

items.mistake: Using BFS on a *weighted* graph and calling the result 'shortest path'.
items.fix: Use Dijkstra (or A*) when edges carry weights.
items.why: BFS minimizes the *number* of edges, not their total weight; a 3-edge path can be cheaper than a 1-edge path with a huge weight.

items.mistake: Running Dijkstra on a graph with negative edge weights.
items.fix: Use Bellman-Ford (slower, O(V·E)) when weights can be negative; restructure to remove negatives if possible.
items.why: Dijkstra's greedy choice assumes that once a node is popped its shortest path is final — only true when adding edges can't reduce cost.
items.example:
```python
# If any weight w < 0: use Bellman-Ford or SPFA, not Dijkstra.
```

items.mistake: Topologically sorting a graph that has cycles.
items.fix: Detect cycles first (DFS with `gray` set) and bail out, or treat un-emitted nodes as the cycle witnesses.
items.why: A cycle means no linear order exists; silently returning a partial order causes downstream pipelines to skip work.
items.example:
```python
if len(order) != len(nodes):
    raise ValueError('cycle detected')
```

items.mistake: Recursing DFS on graphs that may be deep (tens of thousands of nodes).
items.fix: Convert to an iterative DFS with an explicit stack.
items.why: Python's default recursion depth is ~1000; long chains trigger `RecursionError`.
items.example:
```python
def dfs(g, src):
    seen, st = set(), [src]
    while st:
        n = st.pop()
        if n in seen: continue
        seen.add(n)
        st.extend(g[n])
```
